{{Short description|Subatomic particle with no electric charge found in atomic nuclei}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Neutron
| image = Quark structure neutron.svg
| caption = A neutron is composed of one [[up quark]] and two [[down quark]]s, held together by the [[strong interaction]].
| topics = [[Atomic nucleus]], [[Nuclear physics]], [[Proton]], [[Strong interaction]], [[Beta decay]]
| composition = 1 [[up quark]], 2 [[down quark]]s
| statistics = [[Fermion]]
| symbol = n, n⁰
| antiparticle = [[Antineutron]]
| mass = 1.67492749804×10⁻²⁷ kg (939.565 MeV/c²)
| charge = 0
| spin = 1/2
}}

The '''neutron''' is a [[subatomic particle]] with no net [[electric charge]] and a mass slightly larger than that of a [[proton]]. Protons and neutrons constitute the [[atomic nucleus|nuclei]] of [[atom]]s; they are collectively called [[nucleon]]s. The neutron has a mean lifetime of approximately 14 minutes and 39 seconds when free; it decays via the [[weak interaction]] into a proton, an [[electron]], and an [[antineutrino]] ([[beta decay]]).

== Discovery ==
The neutron was discovered in 1932 by [[James Chadwick]], for which he received the [[Nobel Prize in Physics]] in 1935. Chadwick bombarded [[beryllium]] with [[alpha particle]]s from [[polonium]] decay and observed a neutral, penetrating radiation that he identified as neutrons.

== Properties ==

=== Mass and stability ===
The neutron rest mass is 939.565 MeV/c², about 0.14% heavier than the proton (938.272 MeV/c²). This mass difference is the reason free neutrons undergo beta decay:
:<math>n \rightarrow p + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e</math>

The half-life of a free neutron is approximately 10 minutes, but neutrons bound in stable nuclei are stable.

=== Internal structure ===
The neutron is a [[baryon]] composed of one [[up quark]] (charge +2/3e) and two [[down quark]]s (charge −1/3e each), resulting in net zero charge. These [[valence quark]]s are surrounded by a sea of virtual quark–antiquark pairs and [[gluon]]s.

=== Magnetic moment ===
Despite having zero net charge, the neutron has a non-zero [[magnetic moment]]:
:<math>\mu_n = -1.913\,\mu_N</math>
indicating complex internal charge distributions.

== Role in nuclei ==

=== Nuclear stability ===
Neutrons provide the "glue" that holds nuclei together via the [[strong interaction]], contributing nuclear binding without adding to [[electric field|electrostatic repulsion]]. The ratio of neutrons to protons increases with atomic number for stable nuclei.

=== Nuclear reactions ===
* '''[[Nuclear fission]]''' — neutrons induce fission in heavy nuclei
* '''[[Nuclear fusion]]''' — neutrons are released in fusion reactions
* '''[[Neutron activation]]''' — production of radioactive isotopes
* '''[[Nuclear chain reaction]]''' — neutrons sustain reactions in reactors and weapons

== Applications ==
* [[Neutron diffraction]] — studying crystal and molecular structures
* [[Neutron activation analysis]] — elemental analysis
* [[Neutron therapy]] — cancer treatment
* [[Nuclear reactor]]s — energy production

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Atomic nucleus]]
* [[Beta decay]]
* [[Nuclear fission]]
* [[Nuclear physics]]
* [[Proton]]
* [[Quark]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Neutron| ]]
[[Category:Baryons]]
[[Category:Nucleons]]
[[Category:Nuclear physics]]
